question: Q5 - Demonstrate the limitations of the author's views on the i) organizational culture ii) skill set and iii) role required for an experience good through one example each for a b2b service (other than IT services). Each of the 3 examples above should cite a different brand.

answers.brand: Safexpress (Org Culture) + Bureau Veritas India (Skill Set) + Hilti India (Role)
answers.brand_concept: Safexpress operations/reliability-driven culture contradicts Moorthi's innovation-driven; Bureau Veritas technical/analytical skills contradict engaging interest; Hilti productivity consultant role contradicts entertainer
answers.framework: Moorthi (2002) Services Branding Framework — Experience Good
answers.answer_text:
Moorthi (2002) argues for experience goods: (i) culture must be 'innovation driven,' (ii) skills must aim at 'engaging customer's interest and attention,' (iii) role is that of an 'entertainer' — a 'doer and talker.' These prescriptions show limitations when applied to B2B experience goods where reliability, technical rigour, and advisory depth matter more.

i) Organizational Culture — Safexpress (B2B Express Logistics):
Moorthi states culture should be 'innovation driven' since 'making the process exciting and pleasurable is what matters.' However, Safexpress, India's largest B2B express logistics company serving 5,000+ corporates, operates under an operations-and-reliability-driven culture. Their philosophy is 'Custodians First, Carriers Later,' placing consignment safety above innovation. They have 'standardized operations across all 811 gateways and 10,000+ trucks, utilizing GPS tracking for real-time decision-making' [1]. Culture emphasizes 'zero-conflict, no-questions-asked issue resolution, 365-days business continuity' [2]. For B2B logistics — an experience good evaluated after each shipment cycle — culture is driven by operational consistency, not innovation.

ii) Skill Set — Bureau Veritas India (B2B Testing, Inspection & Certification):
Moorthi prescribes skills for 'engaging customer's interest and attention.' Bureau Veritas India — operating 26 laboratories — requires entirely different skills: 'state-of-the-art facilities staffed by master chemists, highly trained technicians and laboratory management with a wealth of industry knowledge' [3]. Skills needed are technical precision, analytical rigour, and regulatory compliance — not customer engagement. A client commissioning a materials compliance test needs scientifically accurate, legally defensible results, not to be 'engaged.'

iii) Role — Hilti India (B2B Construction Tool Fleet Management):
Moorthi states the role is 'entertainer' — 'doer and talker.' Hilti India's Fleet Management service positions the provider as a technical advisor, not entertainer. Hilti deploys 'productivity consultants' who 'help uncover hidden costs, optimize your tool crib and streamline tool crib management processes' through 'onsite productivity analysis' [4]. Their DDS platform provides 'real-time information on utilisation of power tools, including idle assets' [5]. The role is diagnostic problem-solver — analyzing data, advising on cost optimization — not entertaining.

framework_source.section: Organizational Culture / Skill Set Required / Role
framework_source.cite_as: Moorthi, Y.L.R., 'An Approach to Branding Services', Journal of Services Marketing, Vol. 16, No. 3, 2002
framework_source.page_hint: Search for 'innovation driven' and 'entertainer' in the file

answers.brand: Sodexo India (Org Culture) + CRISIL (Skill Set) + Thermax (Role)
answers.brand_concept: Sodexo consistency/compliance-driven culture; CRISIL analytical rigour skills; Thermax silent executor role
answers.framework: Moorthi (2002) Services Branding Framework — Experience Good
answers.answer_text:
i) Organizational Culture — Sodexo India (B2B Corporate Catering):
Moorthi argues culture must be 'innovation driven.' Sodexo India, serving 500+ corporate clients at 1,100+ sites with 40,500 employees, operates a consistency-and-compliance-driven culture. Their model centres on 'standardising recipes for consistency' and 'centralised offsite kitchens that enhance consistency, food safety, and cost efficiency' [1]. Client feedback: 'Sodexo's services score great consistency on all parameters — safety, wellbeing, cleanliness and comfort' [1]. A factory cafeteria needs safe, consistent meals at scale — not exciting innovation. This is operations-driven, contradicting Moorthi.

ii) Skill Set — CRISIL (B2B Credit Rating & Analytics):
Moorthi prescribes skills for 'engaging customer's interest.' CRISIL, India's largest credit rating agency (S&P Global subsidiary), requires 'extraordinary domain expertise and analytical rigour' to help clients 'make mission-critical decisions with confidence' [2]. Rating process ensures 'highest standards of independence and analytical rigour' with 'analysis by a multi-member rating team' [3]. Credit rating is an experience good evaluated after observing accuracy over time. Skills needed: quantitative analysis, financial modelling — not 'engagement.'

iii) Role — Thermax (B2B Industrial Energy Solutions):
Moorthi prescribes the 'entertainer' role. Thermax's TOESL subsidiary operates on Build-Own-Operate model: 'invests, installs and operates a utility plant in customer premises and undertakes comprehensive O&M' [4]. Thermax is 'Trusted Partner in Energy Transition' — 'customers are relieved from burden of owning and running utilities' to 'focus on core manufacturing' [4]. Role = silent executor, behind-the-scenes problem-solver — not entertainer. Client barely interacts if system runs well.

answers.brand: Compass Group India (Org Culture) + Rentokil PCI (Skill Set) + Grundfos India (Role)
answers.brand_concept: Compass Group safety-first/hygiene culture; Rentokil PCI scientific pest analysis skills; Grundfos technical optimizer/diagnostic role
answers.framework: Moorthi (2002) Services Branding Framework — Experience Good
answers.answer_text:
i) Organizational Culture — Compass Group India (B2B Corporate Catering):
Moorthi states culture must be 'innovation driven.' Compass Group India — world's largest food services company, 45+ Indian cities, 1M+ daily meals, 200M+ sq.ft. managed facilities — runs a hygiene-and-safety-first culture. Their 'Safety First approach underpins all corporate facility management solutions,' digital tool SHIELD 'strengthens food safety process, focusing on reducing food safety incidents' [1]. Food services ERP 'streamlines and standardises recipe management and production planning' [1]. Culture is ISO9001-certified compliance-driven, not innovation-driven [2].

ii) Skill Set — Rentokil PCI (B2B Commercial Pest Control):
Moorthi prescribes skills for 'engaging customer's interest.' Rentokil PCI, India's largest B2B pest control company (300+ locations), deploys 'Field Biologists working across India who carry out in-depth examinations and analysis of production processes and pest risks' [3]. Technicians receive 'professional industry-accredited training' through 'Rentokil Technical Academy' focused on 'pest knowledge, understanding rodent behaviour' [3]. Skills = entomological analysis, chemical application, regulatory compliance — opposite of 'engaging customer interest.' A pharma plant wants scientifically rigorous elimination, not engagement.

iii) Role — Grundfos India (B2B Industrial Pumping Solutions):
Moorthi prescribes 'entertainer' role. Grundfos India (600+ employees, 2 manufacturing facilities) acts as technical optimizer: their 'advanced e-solutions programme can help optimize processes and reduce energy consumption up to 50 percent' [5]. Experts provide 'no-obligation consultations to discuss how to optimize operations and system building' [5]. Role = engineering consultant who audits water/energy systems, diagnoses inefficiencies, prescribes solutions — zero entertainment. A cement plant wants pump optimization, not a 'doer and talker.'

chatgpt.response:
For **experience goods**, quality is difficult to evaluate before purchase and becomes clear only after consumption (typical examples: consulting, logistics, facility management, maintenance contracts). Many authors argue that such businesses require:  
• a strong **customer‑centric organizational culture**  
• specialized **relationship and service skills**  
• a **boundary‑spanning relationship manager role** to manage the client experience.  

However, these views can be limited because other structural factors (technology, contracts, standardization, or regulation) may dominate the experience. Examples from B2B services illustrate these limitations.

------------------------------------------------

1) Limitation of the author’s view on **Organizational Culture**

Author’s view:  
Experience goods require a deeply customer‑centric culture because service quality depends heavily on employee behaviour and attitudes.

Limitation:  
In many B2B services, **operational systems and processes** matter more than culture in shaping the customer experience.

Example: **Sodexo India – Integrated Facility Management**

Sodexo provides B2B services such as workplace food services, facility management, and employee benefits to large corporations in India.

Why the author’s view is limited:  
• In large corporate contracts (factories, hospitals, IT campuses), service quality depends heavily on **standard operating procedures, safety protocols, and compliance systems**, not only on employee culture.  
• Clients choose Sodexo primarily because of **process reliability, food safety standards (HACCP), and audit compliance**, which reduce operational risk.  
• Even if organizational culture is positive, a failure in **supply chain logistics or hygiene compliance** can damage the experience.

Thus, the **system design and compliance infrastructure** often dominate over organizational culture in determining perceived service quality.

Evidence:  
• Sodexo highlights standardized food safety and compliance frameworks in its India operations.  
• Large institutional clients prioritize vendor compliance and operational metrics in facility management outsourcing (Sodexo India corporate communications; Economic Times coverage of facility management outsourcing growth).

------------------------------------------------

2) Limitation of the author’s view on **Skill Set**

Author’s view:  
Employees in experience goods need strong interpersonal and relationship skills because the service experience depends on human interaction.

Limitation:  
In many B2B services, **technical infrastructure and network capability** are more important than interpersonal service skills.

Example: **Blue Dart (DHL Group) – B2B Logistics and Express Delivery**

Blue Dart provides logistics services to businesses such as pharmaceutical companies, banks, and e‑commerce sellers.

Why the author’s view is limited:  
• The customer experience is largely determined by **logistics infrastructure**, including aircraft fleet, hub‑and‑spoke network, and real‑time tracking systems.  
• Clients value **delivery reliability, transit time predictability, and tracking visibility** more than interpersonal service skills.  
• Even if employees have excellent service skills, poor routing algorithms or delayed flights will damage the experience.

Therefore, in logistics, **operations analytics, supply chain engineering, and digital tracking capabilities** are often more critical than traditional service skills.

Evidence:  
• Blue Dart emphasizes its **dedicated cargo airline, automated hubs, and tracking systems** as key differentiators.  
• DHL and Blue Dart report that network infrastructure and technology drive service reliability (Blue Dart annual reports; logistics industry analyses in Business Standard and Economic Times).

------------------------------------------------

3) Limitation of the author’s view on **Role (Relationship Manager / Boundary Spanner)**

Author’s view:  
For experience goods, firms must appoint a **relationship manager** who coordinates service delivery and manages client expectations.

Limitation:  
In some B2B services, **long‑term contracts and productized service systems** reduce the importance of individual relationship roles.

Example: **Schindler India – Elevator Maintenance Services**

Schindler provides elevator installation and maintenance contracts to commercial buildings, hospitals, and real estate developers.

Why the author’s view is limited:  
• The customer experience depends mainly on **preventive maintenance technology, IoT monitoring, and uptime guarantees**, rather than personal relationship management.  
• Elevator systems increasingly use **predictive maintenance platforms (remote monitoring sensors)** that automatically schedule service.  
• The client interacts more with **digital dashboards and service tickets** than with a relationship manager.

Thus, the service experience is increasingly **technology‑managed**, reducing the importance of the traditional boundary‑spanning relationship role.

Evidence:  
• Schindler’s digital platform “Schindler Ahead” uses IoT sensors for predictive maintenance and real‑time performance monitoring.  
• Smart elevator maintenance systems shift value creation from personal relationships to **data‑driven uptime management** (Schindler India product documentation; industry coverage in Construction Week India).

------------------------------------------------

Conclusion

These examples show that the author’s assumptions about experience goods can be **overly human‑centric**. In many modern B2B services:

• **Systems and compliance** may outweigh organizational culture (Sodexo).  
• **Infrastructure and analytics capabilities** may matter more than interpersonal skill sets (Blue Dart).  
• **Technology platforms and automated monitoring** can reduce the importance of relationship manager roles (Schindler).

Therefore, while culture, skills, and roles remain important, **technology, processes, and contractual structures increasingly shape the experience in B2B service markets**.
